slides.kicker: // sample template · e-learning and online education
slides.lead: This is a demo (sample) template. All text is dummy content and exists only to showcase the styles.
slides.notes: Opening slide. Put the event title, a one-line subtitle and a meta row at the bottom here. Highlight one word with em for the accent color.

slides.lead: Five roles, one shared goal: better learning.
slides.notes: Team slide. Instead of photos it uses initials in a colored circle, so it needs no images.

slides.lead: Three parts, with room for questions between them.
slides.notes: Agenda as three large tiles. Good at the start of a block.

slides.lead: Engagement, outcomes and the biggest drop-off points.
slides.notes: Divider slide between blocks. Large heading on a colored gradient.

slides.lead: Courses, videos and articles are abundant. The scarce resource is sustained learner attention, and that is where design wins.
slides.footnote: // illustrative slide with an enlarged image
slides.notes: Hero slide: large heading, a bold illustration, a short caption. Good for a key point or chapter open.

items: higher course completion after redesigning the first week
saved weekly per instructor on manual grading
more active learners with cohort-based pacing
bullets: Measure outcomes, not logins: completion and skill gain beat raw traffic.
Start where learners already drop off and fix that step first.
A small cohort with a clear metric beats a huge launch without one.
slides.notes: KPI dashboard: three cards with an icon, a big number and a caption, with supporting bullets below.

slides.lead: The path bends to the learner, not the other way around.
bullets: What each learner already knows and where they stall.
Difficulty and pace to keep the challenge just right.
With timely reminders before motivation fades.
Progress that mentors can actually act on.
slides.notes: Two-column slide: bullets with a strong lead word on the left, an illustration on the right. Text scales itself.

slides.lead: Same content, two formats and two very different finish rates.
cards: Learn anytime. Flexible, but completion often quietly stalls.
Fixed weekly releases. More rhythm, less personal flexibility.
Learn together on a shared timeline. Highest completion.
Live sessions plus self-paced work between them.
slides.notes: A 2x2 grid of cards. Good for a comparison or a list of variants.

slides.lead: Six typical building blocks of a modern learning platform.
cards: Author lessons, quizzes and paths in one place.
Real-time view of where every cohort stands.
Flag stalling learners before they quietly leave.
Tie each lesson to a concrete, assessable skill.
Pair learners with a human who checks in.
Learner data stays governed and under control.
slides.notes: A 3x2 grid with an icon in each card header. Great for an overview of tools or capabilities.

items: Completion levels off, so it pays to polish the fundamentals.
Adaptive tools improve fast and reshape established courses.
Automation starts drafting the lessons, not just grading them.
slides.notes: Numbered cards with small trend charts and an illustration on the right. Ideal for three possible futures.

slides.lead: Five steps, each with a clear deliverable.
slides.notes: A horizontal flow of steps with arrows. Each box has a heading and a small caption.

slides.quote: The best course is not the one with the most lessons, but the one learners actually finish.
slides.by: Sample quote to showcase the style
slides.notes: Quote slide with a bold left rule. Keep the attribution small.

slides.lead: Simple, but easy to forget under deadline pressure.
bullets: Every course has the name of a person accountable for it.
You decide upfront how you will know it worked.
For high-stakes feedback, a mentor always reviews.
slides.notes: A plain bullet slide with a strong lead word. Text scales up automatically to fill the space.

slides.lead: A course no one completes is a cost, not an outcome.
slides.notes: Statement slide: one large sentence centered on the page. Use it for a key message.

slides.lead: What to do this week, this month and this quarter.
slides.notes: Second divider, opening the practical part.

slides.lead: We switch into the platform and build a lesson live.
slides.footnote: // placeholder slide for a demo or break
slides.notes: Placeholder slide. Centered heading, ideal for a live demo, a break or questions.
Closing slide in the style of the title slide. Contact and a call to action.
